display_title: 1985-03_04 — BEARING 315 Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club Mar/Apr 1985
body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club
Mar/Apr 1985

Volunteer of the Year
First of all a hearty thanks to thw club members who have volunteered their time to help at the last couple meets. As you can see in the "Results" section of the newsletter, there are a number of new names of people helping at meets besides the old standbys. I think I can speak on behalf of the old standbys in saying that we are happy to see the new faces helping at the meets.

What better way is there to become involved with the club and improve "O" skills than to help out at the meets. Harvey Friedman prefers registration duties because then he can place names with faces. I myself prefer course setting as it helps me improve my map reading skills. After helping at several meets you'll end up as part of the inner circle that I see in the club. You'll get tired of running in Woodland Park for the umteenth time and welcome the challenge of setting a course or doing some field checking. You may even get to the point of planning your vacation trip around an "O" meet in the U.S., Canada, or even Europe.

Granted, there are a lot of headaches such as waiting on I-90 for the road to clear or picking up control markers in a cold downpour but it will give you something to remember. And of course the most disheartening time is when people complain about a misplaced control on a course you set. But hopefully you will have learned from this experience and be a little more sympathetic when you run a course with a misplaced control marker.

During the rest of the year I hope to keep track of who has helped at the meets. I hope to recognize the "Volunteer of the Year" in some way such as a years free membership in the Club or USOF, a compass, a pair of running shoes, an "O" suit, the Vice-Presidency or what ever I can finagle from the BOD. Points would be assigned according to what you did; i. e., course setter 3 pts., meet director 2 pts., all other work 1 pt. Taking our 1st 2 meets as an example you would have a tie for first with Jan Vrana and Sture Carlsson each with 5 pts. and a tie for 3rd between Kathryn Risch and Arnold Kay each with 2 pts. So think about it and give me a call; hope to have the guidelines spelled out in the next issue of BEARING 315.

  • John Huth 522-1304

P. S. If you cannot help at meets we can still use your help. If you'll notice on the inside cover of BEARING 315, there is a list of coordinators on the BOD. Give one a call to help out or give President Debbie Newell (488-7471) a call to volunteer for a vacant coordinator position. If you can't do any of the above, we can still use your help. Introduce a new person to orienteering whenever you can. That's right, drag a relative, friend, acquaintance, or stranger to a meet every time you attend. Perhaps we can have a contest for most new members too. -J.H.

BEARING 315 contains results, a schedule, articles of interest contributed by members or selected from other sources and lots of material written by the editor, Harvey Friedman; letters to the editor are most welcome. Any correspondence to Cascade Orienteering Club or to this Newsletter should go to

P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, Wash. 98103

Other clubs wishing to receive BEARING 315 in exchange should send their newsletters directly to the above address.

Next Issue
All material to be published in the next issue must be received by 15 April, the earlier, the better.

Club dues
Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Rick Breseman, Melissa Bridges, Stephanie Colony, Don Denbo, Ardis Dull, Scott Elich, Faith & Michel Fich, the Firlotte family, Bill Fritz family, Halvorsen family, Joe Hudson, Peter Kehoe, Barbara Maly, Gary McCausland, Robert & Sandy Miller, Carl Moore, Harry Morgan, Dennis Mosolf, Janet Piehl, Susan St. John, Mike Schuh, Tom & Gloria Schuh, Roger Sharp, JoAnne & Will Sperry, Valerie Tinsley, the Waughs, Miriam Weber, Ken Wiljanen.

SCHEDULE OF EVENTS

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

Carkeek Park
2 February 1985

We had a good turnout, on a bright sunny day, to run the courses designed and set by Anne York. We also enjoyed the visit of 5 men from the British Army staying in Fort Lewis on maneuvers.
Thanks to Anne for course setting, Ardis Dull for start timing, Arnold Kay for finish timing, Harvey Friedman, Tim Vargo, Joe Hudson, and Robert Miller for picking up control markers, and Debbie Newell and Jan Vrana for pitching in.
-- Sandy Kish Miller, Meet Director and Registrar

Pot-O'-luck
The next Cascade O.C. potluck will be at Jennifer Seffernick's at 7507-55th N.E. in Seattle at 6:30 on 29 March. Phone 522-0752. As usually occurs at these potlucks, the food should again be excellent and you have the opportunity to socialize and talk about something other than orienteering ( or you can talk about orienteering ).

Course Setting Class
Mike Schuh will a short session in the fine art of course setting in mid-March. Tentative dates are the weekends of 16 and 23 March. This session is intended for everyone who will be setting courses for club events this year. Topics will include how to design a course, techniques of marker placement, writing descriptions and use of international symbols, how to estimate winning times, and discussion of several "No-no's". If you are interested in attending, give Mike a call (home 783-3960 work 356-6241) or talk to him at a club event or at the club meeting, 4 March.

  • Mike Schuh

Page 11

Field-Checking Class
Word from the north is that there will be a class on mapping during the 30-31 March week-end around Nanaimo, B. C. led by Afan Jones and Mark Fettes, both experienced mappers. Phone Colin at 604-251-9104 for more info.

Cascade Chatter
It's hard to say who was more surprised at the November pot-luck when Knut Olson and Len Englund recognized each other as having run in the same meets about 40 years ago in Sweden. Seems that they both came to the Seattle area at about the same time in the early 50's but didn't know the other was here. If only those 2 dynamic individuals had gotten together then when they were younger, they probably would have founded an O club that is much stronger than any in the country today. O, well - at least they are Cascade members today....Speaking of Knut Olson, when he ran a course at the Farrel-McWhirter meet 19 January 1985, that was the 1st O course he had attempted since about 1950....We had a new record for WIOL participation at the Carkeek Park meet 2 February 1985 but it was not for students, (alas), but rather for parents or coaches of competitors (hooray). Marilyn Firlotte, M. C. Halvorsen, Lowell Cline, Phil Everett and coach Genest all found a great way to stay warm while waiting for the results of the WIOL course....The latest word is that the Nisqually Orienteers Class A meet will be held in late September not late August or early September as we had been told....The U.S. Intercollegiate Champs ( but as are most O meets in this country, open to anyone) will be held 30-31 March in Birmingham, Alabama by Vulcan O Club....For those who want to be in the same woods with the super-competitive, 19-21 April 1985 is the date for the U.S. Team Trials to select the team to represent the U.S. in the world championships in September in Australia. I have some applications for it....There will be an article on O in "Sunset" magazine, either the April or May issue; if you were at Farrel-McWhirter park, your picture might be in there....For all who complain that our local parks don't give us enough area to practice rough orienteering skills be reminded of the Class B meet 16 March at Durr Road in Ellensburg. Take either I-90 exit and find the McDonalds restaurant on Main St. Take the road opposite McD's and follow it thru its turns and name changes until it becomes Durr Road where you'll find an O sign. Easier than it sounds. Don't forget your compass; you'll need it....For more stuff in this column, you all will have to inform me about what you're doing - even if it is an anonymous note!....

Where were they?
'Twas a disappointingly small turnout of students for the 3rd annual WIOL state championship meet in 1985. Only 27 of the over 40 folks eligible for the Seward Park meet were there. Anyone with ideas for improving participation and the time to implement your ideas (with help of course), please write them up. It is not too early to be thinking about a WIOL coordinator for next year. -HF

block_index.text: BEARING 315 Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club Mar/Apr 1985

Volunteer of the Year First of all a hearty thanks to thw club members who have volunteered their time to help at the last couple meets. As you can see in the "Results" section of the newsletter, there are a number of new names of people helping at meets besides the old standbys. I think I can speak on behalf of the old standbys in saying that we are happy to see the new faces helping at the meets.

What better way is there to become involved with the club and improve "O" skills than to help out at the meets. Harvey Friedman prefers registration duties because then he can place names with faces. I myself prefer course setting as it helps me improve my map reading skills. After helping at several meets you'll end up as part of the inner circle that I see in the club. You'll get tired of running in Woodland Park for the umteenth time and welcome the challenge of setting a course or doing some field checking. You may even get to the point of planning your vacation trip around an "O" meet in the U.S., Canada, or even Europe.

Granted, there are a lot of headaches such as waiting on I-90 for the road to clear or picking up control markers in a cold downpour but it will give you something to remember. And of course the most disheartening time is when people complain about a misplaced control on a course you set. But hopefully you will have learned from this experience and be a little more sympathetic when you run a course with a misplaced control marker.

During the rest of the year I hope to keep track of who has helped at the meets. I hope to recognize the "Volunteer of the Year" in some way such as a years free membership in the Club or USOF, a compass, a pair of running shoes, an "O" suit, the Vice-Presidency or what ever I can finagle from the BOD. Points would be assigned according to what you did; i. e., course setter 3 pts., meet director 2 pts., all other work 1 pt. Taking our 1st 2 meets as an example you would have a tie for first with Jan Vrana and Sture Carlsson each with 5 pts. and a tie for 3rd between Kathryn Risch and Arnold Kay each with 2 pts. So think about it and give me a call; hope to have the guidelines spelled out in the next issue of BEARING 315.

P. S. If you cannot help at meets we can still use your help. If you'll notice on the inside cover of BEARING 315, there is a list of coordinators on the BOD. Give one a call to help out or give President Debbie Newell (488-7471) a call to volunteer for a vacant coordinator position. If you can't do any of the above, we can still use your help. Introduce a new person to orienteering whenever you can. That's right, drag a relative, friend, acquaintance, or stranger to a meet every time you attend. Perhaps we can have a contest for most new members too. -J.H.

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.
University Branch Library, 488-7471. 5009 Roosevelt Way N. E., Seattle. "Summer meets, here & there". Bring your compass.
Exit off of I-90 East of Snoqualmie Pass, 1:15000 b&w. registration 11:00 to 12:00. Another try at orienteering on skinny skis. Call 522-1304 or LA4-3437 for more info and parking instructions.

We had a good turnout, on a bright sunny day, to run the courses designed and set by Anne York. We also enjoyed the visit of 5 men from the British Army staying in Fort Lewis on maneuvers. Thanks to Anne for course setting, Ardis Dull for start timing, Arnold Kay for finish timing, Harvey Friedman, Tim Vargo, Joe Hudson, and Robert Miller for picking up control markers, and Debbie Newell and Jan Vrana for pitching in. -- Sandy Kish Miller, Meet Director and Registrar

A special acknowledgement is due the following people who assisted at this years WIOL Championship meet. Dave Enger, Harvey Friedman, Joe Hudson, Arnold Kay, Sandra Kish Miller, Gary McCausland, Robert Miller, Debbie Newell, Mike Schuh, Francis Smith, Nathan Tharp, Jan Vrana, Dan Waugh, and anyone else I may have missed.

Pot-O'-luck The next Cascade O.C. potluck will be at Jennifer Seffernick's at 7507-55th N.E. in Seattle at 6:30 on 29 March. Phone 522-0752. As usually occurs at these potlucks, the food should again be excellent and you have the opportunity to socialize and talk about something other than orienteering ( or you can talk about orienteering ).

Course Setting Class Mike Schuh will a short session in the fine art of course setting in mid-March. Tentative dates are the weekends of 16 and 23 March. This session is intended for everyone who will be setting courses for club events this year. Topics will include how to design a course, techniques of marker placement, writing descriptions and use of international symbols, how to estimate winning times, and discussion of several "No-no's". If you are interested in attending, give Mike a call (home 783-3960 work 356-6241) or talk to him at a club event or at the club meeting, 4 March.

Field-Checking Class Word from the north is that there will be a class on mapping during the 30-31 March week-end around Nanaimo, B. C. led by Afan Jones and Mark Fettes, both experienced mappers. Phone Colin at 604-251-9104 for more info.

Cascade Chatter It's hard to say who was more surprised at the November pot-luck when Knut Olson and Len Englund recognized each other as having run in the same meets about 40 years ago in Sweden. Seems that they both came to the Seattle area at about the same time in the early 50's but didn't know the other was here. If only those 2 dynamic individuals had gotten together then when they were younger, they probably would have founded an O club that is much stronger than any in the country today. O, well - at least they are Cascade members today....Speaking of Knut Olson, when he ran a course at the Farrel-McWhirter meet 19 January 1985, that was the 1st O course he had attempted since about 1950....We had a new record for WIOL participation at the Carkeek Park meet 2 February 1985 but it was not for students, (alas), but rather for parents or coaches of competitors (hooray). Marilyn Firlotte, M. C. Halvorsen, Lowell Cline, Phil Everett and coach Genest all found a great way to stay warm while waiting for the results of the WIOL course....The latest word is that the Nisqually Orienteers Class A meet will be held in late September not late August or early September as we had been told....The U.S. Intercollegiate Champs ( but as are most O meets in this country, open to anyone) will be held 30-31 March in Birmingham, Alabama by Vulcan O Club....For those who want to be in the same woods with the super-competitive, 19-21 April 1985 is the date for the U.S. Team Trials to select the team to represent the U.S. in the world championships in September in Australia. I have some applications for it....There will be an article on O in "Sunset" magazine, either the April or May issue; if you were at Farrel-McWhirter park, your picture might be in there....For all who complain that our local parks don't give us enough area to practice rough orienteering skills be reminded of the Class B meet 16 March at Durr Road in Ellensburg. Take either I-90 exit and find the McDonalds restaurant on Main St. Take the road opposite McD's and follow it thru its turns and name changes until it becomes Durr Road where you'll find an O sign. Easier than it sounds. Don't forget your compass; you'll need it....For more stuff in this column, you all will have to inform me about what you're doing - even if it is an anonymous note!....

Where were they? 'Twas a disappointingly small turnout of students for the 3rd annual WIOL state championship meet in 1985. Only 27 of the over 40 folks eligible for the Seward Park meet were there. Anyone with ideas for improving participation and the time to implement your ideas (with help of course), please write them up. It is not too early to be thinking about a WIOL coordinator for next year. -HF
